data.title: Rio Tinto: Up A Lot, Still A Good Outlook
data.description:
Article Thesis

Rio Tinto Group (RIO) is one of the largest mining companies in the world and benefits from growing demand for a range of commodities. Shares have performed well over the last year but aren't expensive, and profits are forecasted to grow nicely this year. For investors seeking a nice dividend yield and exposure to global commodity and base materials markets, Rio Tinto remains a compelling choice.

Past Coverage

I have written about Rio Tinto Group here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in August 2024. I gave the company a Buy rating back then, arguing that shares traded at a too-low valuation, while Rio Tinto also benefited from several macro trends. This call worked out well so far, as Rio Tinto has returned a little more than 40% since that article was published, versus a return of a little more than 20% from the broad market over the same time frame. With more than a year having passed since my last update on Rio Tinto, and with several news items that are of importance for the company coming out in January, it is time for me to update my views on the company today.

Rio Tinto: Relevant News Items

The resource industries generally are capital intense, and bringing on new production can easily cost billions, while greenfield projects also can take many years from the decision to pursue them until they start producing. It is thus not surprising that growing via acquisitions and mergers is an often-used approach for players in these industries. Rio Tinto is a company that has engaged in M&A repeatedly in the past, and we recently got the news that RIO is talking about a deal with Glencore (GLCNF, GLNCY).

There is, for now, no guarantee that a deal will happen, and we also do not yet know what such a deal would look like -- a full-on merger is a possibility, but it's also possible that RIO will only acquire some of the assets currently owned by Glencore. If a deal happens, that could be quite positive for Rio Tinto -- depending on the valuation at which it happens, and depending on what synergies Rio Tinto would be able to capture over time.

Rio Tinto has a pretty clean balance sheet, with a net debt position of $14 billion -- around half of this year's expected EBITDA -- so it would not be hard for Rio Tinto to finance a deal, although an all-out merger with Glencore would likely require a stock-for-stock component. It makes sense for investors to watch for potential deal announcements in the coming months, as a well-crafted deal could be a driver for Rio Tinto and its shares in the future.

Rio Tinto also recently announced a deal with Amazon.com (AMZN) that will see RIO supply the latter with especially clean/ environmentally friendly copper. Amazon, as one of the largest data center operators in the world that keeps investing many billions of dollars in new data centers every year, needs large amounts of copper. With this deal, Amazon.com will be the first customer of RIO's "clean copper" that is produced by its Nuton subsidiary. While this deal alone will, I believe, not have a huge impact on RIO's fundamentals yet, I think that this deal shows that demand for cleaner/greener commodities exists, so this business could grow considerably over time when RIO starts selling clean copper to other companies, too.

We also got some politics news that could be of relevance for Rio Tinto. A group of lawmakers across both parties introduced a bill that would see the U.S. stockpile a range of critical commodities. No matter whether that bill ultimately gets passed, I think this shows that politicians across many countries are increasingly seeing the importance of reliable commodity supply, having reserves, being able to supply the country's needs from secure sources, and so on. Overall, this should result in more support for mining companies like Rio Tinto, I believe, and could help them with future project approvals, regulation, and so on -- many countries, including the U.S., want to become less dependent on at-risk suppliers.

Rio Tinto: Performance And Outlook

This week, Rio Tinto also announced how it performed during the most recent quarter when it comes to growing its production volumes. The company was able to hit a new record level of production during the most recent quarter, beating a record that had been in place for almost a decade. Almost 91 million tons, across different commodities, were delivered in the December quarter. Annualized, that's more than 360 million tons -- well above the result for the entire year, which was around 330 million tons. This shows that there was some acceleration throughout the last year, which, of course, is positive. This was made possible by an especially strong iron ore performance from Rio Tinto, as the company benefited from healthy demand in China -- with recently improving iron ore pricing also indicating that demand is far from bad:

In the above chart, we see that iron ore (SCO:COM) prices firmed noticeably over the last half year, and this trend may remain in place as 2026 is forecasted to be a good year for economic growth across the U.S., India, China, and so on.

It is thus, I believe, not surprising to see that analysts are forecasting that Rio Tinto will see appealing earnings growth this year: Relative to the forecasted earnings per share of $6.55 in 2025, 2026's earnings per share are forecasted to grow by around 12% to a little north of $7.30. While far from Nvidia-like (NVDA) growth, a double-digit growth rate is quite compelling, especially when combined with a nice dividend yield and a valuation that isn't overly high -- and that's the case with Rio Tinto.

Rio Tinto: Yield And Valuation

At current prices, Rio Tinto offers a dividend yield of around 4.5%, which is pretty good. That being said, RIO has a history of adjusting its dividend to its profitability level over time -- this has resulted in dividend reductions at times, so the dividend could easily decline should earnings pull back. Since profits are forecasted to grow this year, however, I do think that the near-term outlook for the dividend is good -- but investors should expect a (temporary) dividend reduction in case commodity markets were to take a hit.

Based on the forecasted earnings per share for 2026, Rio Tinto currently trades at an earnings multiple of around 12 -- not the lowest valuation it has traded at, but also far from a high valuation. Looking at Rio Tinto's enterprise value to EBITDA multiple, we see the following:

Rio Tinto trades marginally below the longer-term average, and a 5x to 6x EBITDA multiple also seems far from expensive in absolute terms. This underlines my belief that Rio Tinto is a very solid value, although not a mega-bargain, at current prices.

Takeaway

Should the world enter a recession, commodity demand would take a hit, and so would RIO's profitability, and the dividend would likely get reduced in such a scenario. But I think that a recession isn't very likely in the near term -- for now, the outlook for Rio Tinto is good.

Strong iron ore production levels and firming prices should be good for Rio Tinto in 2026, and M&A, "green commodity" ventures, etc. provide growth potential, too. For someone looking for commodity exposure, Rio Tinto looks attractive, I believe.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Three Ways To Play The Rexford Recovery
data.description:
Rexford (REXR) seems to be in recovery mode, after a challenging year for investors in the SoCal Industrial REIT.

REXR assets are highly focused in the L.A. Port area, and thus exposed to the West Coast import/export trade, which has faced disruptions and uncertainties from the tariff push, specifically in the China and South East Asia trade. This has created significant volatility in the share price in 2025.

My thesis is that Rexford is set to continue to grow earnings in the low single digits, and see mid single-digit price appreciation. The share price has moved from being over-valued to the low end of fair value.

I do not see REXR returning to previous highs, but rather a gradual improvement over time.

In this scenario, the best risk adjusted returns might be achieved by alternatives to simply buying the stock.

The Price and Value Story

I am going to use a few charts to discuss the valuation story for REXR, which I see as fundamental to the investment case here, so bear with me.

The one year performance looks like a good future of a ‘taper tantrum’ drop, followed by a return to business as usual. However, zooming out shows a longer term valuation reset story. The five-year share price chart shows a spike in the share price to over $80 in 2022, followed by a long decline. I was struck by how closely the share price mirrors the price to earnings ratio, each hints at a valuation driven reset.

This valuation story is conducted by the FFO per share performance of the company, which shows steady progress. The inverse correlation between share price and FFO per share since 2023 is quite remarkable.

REXR shows all the signs of great company that was overbought and has been experiencing appropriate multiple reversion.

The company is a favorite on Seeking Alpha, where analysts point to the high quality portfolio of industrial assets, strategic locations, and high quality management. Historical growth has been strong. This has driven the valuation, despite the concentration risk in California.

Peer Comparison

This five year valuation reset can be seen in the price to earnings ratio, where REXR has dropped down from the stratosphere to align with the valuation of peer STAG Industrial (STAG) while now lagging Prologis (PLD).

I’d use a price to AFFO or FFO chart but Y Charts doesn’t offer this, so I am using price to earnings as a directional proxy.

For an FFO based view, I calculated the PFFO for the three at the start and end of the period. You can see that the multiple compression was across the board, averaging about 40%.

REXR's multiple dropped more like 60%, and is now barely at a premium to STAG.

On an FFO per share basis, however, REXR is performing better than both peers, with FFO per share of close to 65% over the last five years. Note how this is just out-stripped PLD, and with a much steadier glide-path. The slow and steady FFO per share growth of REXR looks appealing to me. STAG isn’t even in the running on this metric.

Investor Returns

REXR investors have certainty felt the pain over the last five years, with a 14% drop in the share price vs PLD 36% increase, and STAG 22% rise.

On a total return basis REXR investors have nearly broken even, while STAG and PLD have returns over 50%.

In my mind, the story is pretty clear. Despite concerns around the California concentration, and tariff impact, REXR has been quietly and steadily growing its profits. Meanwhile the price multiple expanded egregiously and has now mean reverted to a sensible level, making REXR a viable investment again. However, the long term price momentum is still downward, indicating that REXR might be a value trap. Investors should understand what is behind this.

Business Performance & Outlook

As discussed, REXR has been growing FFO per share, which is a positive sign. But what is going on underneath that headline?

The year on year numbers in the recent Q3 results show growth in all major KPIs.

The company tightened 2025 guidance towards the higher end of the previous range. The same property cash NOI growth was uplifted by 150bps.

One problem arises due to the competitive landscape on SoCal. While the Rexford portfolio is rented at a premium to the average market rent, lease renewals are eroding this. So year over year, rents are down by 9%.

The mark to market premium is eroding steadily quarter by quarter. Q1 mark to market was 20%, Q2 15%, Q3 13%.

The portfolio has grown, which compensates for the margin erosion, up to 3.3 million square feet. Much however is in redevelopment, so overall occupancy year on year is down by 100bps to 91.8%.

This balances out to a mixed bag. Year to date, the total portfolio NOI growth is 7.7%, but slowing to 2.9% for the latest quarter. The same property stats for the quarter are better than the portfolio level, which is positive. However the drag from properties in redevelopment is tangible.

This translates to a Q3 core FFO per share growth of 1.7%, reversing the drop of 1.7% in the same metric that was reported in Q2.

The question is how the future will play out in the mix between declining rentals and mark to market rental premium, increasing leasable sq.ft., and new leasing activity.

Wall St expects this to be a small FFO gain into 2026, equating to about 2.5% growth over 2025 estimates. Price to FFO estimates are from 17x to 16.7x.

The average price target is $44, with an upside of 7.2% The high estimate is $50, and low estimate $40.

Balance Sheet

REXR has a decent balance with a debt to EBITDA multiple of 4.1x. The rating agencies allocate an investment grade credit rating - BBB+ from S&P.

One concern is that there is a lot of debt outstanding, with a short 3.5 year weighted maturity. REXR have to refinance a lot by the end of 2027.

The current weighted cost of debt for REXR is 4.55% according to Valueinvestor.

Rates have been falling, but the outlook is for longer term rates to rise. The five-year seems most appropriate for REXR given the short maturity profile. Five year rates for 2027 are expected to be around 4% which implies that REXR refinancing costs are likely to be higher than the current cost of debt.

How To Play REXR

Buy and Hold

I last covered REXR in March in an article against the run of opinion. I recommended taking a cautious approach hedged with an options strategy.

Since then, REXR's share price tanked and then recovered. Investors who bought and held REXR through have managed a decent 9% total return. Not bad.

Taking the same approach today, and assuming the consensus price target of $44 is reached, after the 4% dividend, investors would make a total return of 11%. There's also the possibility of a higher share price boosting total returns.

Of course this comes with all the risks of owning stocks, and no downside protection. There is no clear path to dividend growth of more than say 5%. For income investors, this implies a yield staying in the 4% range.

Preferred Shares

For conservative income focused investors, Rexford offers some interesting preferred shares.

Rexford has two outstanding preferred shares to consider, (REXR.PR.B) and (REXR.PR.C).

REXR.PR.B was issued in Nov 2017 with a coupon rate of 5.88%. The preferred now trades below par, generating a yield of 6.5%. The dynamic with preferred shares is that they price more as fixed income products. This means that the preferred shares usually drop when the common shares drop, but by much less - the max 2025 drawdown was around 5%. They are more sensitive to interest rates.

REXR.PR.B has passed the call date, so could be called at any time at par of $25, which would offer upside of around 11%.

REXR has a relatively conservative debt to equity leverage ratio of 0.37x. This implies that they have some room for increasing the debt. The preferred share dividends cost REXR $9.2m annually. Interest expenses are around $100m.

I see it as a possibility that REXR might retire the preferred shares and save the incremental dividend expense in the spread between 5.88% and the cost of new issuance. I don't however see this as likely.

More likely is that investors will receive the 6.5% preferred dividend from a BBB+ rated sponsor.

The preferred dividend is protected by roughly $350m in common dividends.

REXR.PR.C's was issued in September 2019, with 5.63% coupon rate. This is also callable at any time. Dynamics are very similar to REXR.PR.B.

So the preferred shares offer a fixed income equivalent of 6.6%, a decent premium over the common dividend yield of 4%, in return for limited, if any price upside in the absence of rates falling significantly.

Conservative Options Strategy

Regular followers will be aware that I use a conservative options strategy to boost income/hedge downside risk. As discussed above, I have suggested this approach with REXR.

I do back my own suggestions - here are the trades that I have made on REXR in 2025. In March I initiated a $40 covered call, which was called away in April. I then pivoted to selling $40 puts, which were assigned. Back to $40 calls.

In aggregate, these trades have returned 14.5% in options premiums, about 18% including interest and dividends in just under ten months. 22.5% annualised.

I rolled the Jan 16 calls out to April.

A new trade targeting similar returns would be as follows:

Buy 100 REXR @$41 - Sell 1 Jul 26 call @$1.30.

If the share price by July 18 exceeds $45 - the shares will be called for a profit of 15%, or 30% annualised.

If the share price is between $41 and $45, the call will lapse. Income will be 3.2% in option premium plus 2% in dividends - 10.4% annualised at $41.

Compared to a buy and hold investor, the return is 6.4% more in all scenarios other than the share price exceeding $45.

In the event that the price exceeds $44 before July 18, the option can be rolled out and up.

Conclusion

REXR is a high quality business which has been over-priced in the past. The valuation has now reverted to a more reasonable level, and offers some upside. While the business does have some challenges in terms of rent competition it seems to be managing to deliver low single digit earnings growth.

This should continue in 2026. From 2027 onwards, debt refinancing might create some headwinds.

All in all I see REXR as likely to trade between the current price of $41 and $45 in 2026. The Wall Street consensus is $44.

Investors who see more downside than upside risk can buy the preferred shares for a 6.5% income stream.

Investors who think that the share price will exceed $45 might prefer to take the lower 4% yield of the common shares in return for the price upside.

Those who agree with the $41-$45 range could consider an options trade as outlined above which returns between 10.4% and 30% annualised. This trade is similar to my 2025 trading strategy which returned 22%.

data.title: Verisk Analytics: Mediocre Growth And Negative Sentiment To Persist, I'll Pass
data.description:
Introduction

Verisk Analytics, Inc. (VRSK) recently saw its share price decline by a third, which made me take a look at it in more detail to see if there were any reasons for such a performance operationally and whether it would be a good time to start a position. It seems to me that the company is caught in a negative sentiment environment for now, as its products didn’t perform as well as they could have given the AI hype story.

Even after such a decline, I don’t think it’s a good time to start a position and will look for an even better entry point down the road.

It’s been exactly two years since my first article on Verisk, and my sentiment remains unchanged. Nothing is truly exciting regarding its operations. The company is profitable, but it seems not to be sufficient to drive stock price appreciation right now. I maintain a Hold rating.

Performance

Over the past two years, the company’s sales have been steadily increasing, at an average quarterly rate of around 7%, with the underwriting revenue segment continuing to dominate and lead the results forward, while claims growing at around 3%-4% annually. It’s steady growth, but I would say far from impressive, considering the company continues to demand over 32x forward P/E ratio.

For such a P/E ratio, I would expect growth numbers to be at least in the mid-teens. Let’s see what kind of efficiency and profitability the company managed to achieve over the same period. We can see that margins across the board have improved slightly, anywhere from around 200bps to almost 300bps, which is quite good considering the company’s margins are already very attractive, which could also explain why it trades at such a high P/E ratio. It’s an extremely profitable business that is extremely scalable.

Looking at other key metrics in efficiency and profitability, we can see the company has an absurd return on equity, which stems from its financial leverage, which, as of the latest quarter, was 16.5x. ROA and ROTC are both quite attractive, with ROTC standing out as it is almost double what I like to see, which is at least 10%. This tells me the company has a decent moat and pricing power. It is worth noting that these have been trending down recently, so I will need to monitor what happens over the next couple of quarters.

So, let’s get back to the debt situation that affects the company’s ROE. As of the latest quarter, the company had around $2.1B in cash and equivalents against $1.5B in current debt, and $3.2B in long-term debt. Compared to the equity value of $377m, we get a lot of financial leverage. But is it worrisome? Not at all. The company has plenty of cash to pay interest expense on the debt, which was around $42m in the most recent quarter. Its interest coverage ratio was around 8x, which is more than healthy, and with such high margins, the company’s operating cash flow is more than enough to cover these expenses. Even after capex spending, we get free cash flow of $243m. The company has no issues regarding liquidity and solvency. VRSK’s balance sheet may look overleveraged, but I consider it to be rather formidable.

The company is not riding the AI wave like the rest

It seems to me that whenever anyone even slightly mentions AI regarding internal operations, their stock prices skyrocket. This wasn’t the case at all for VRSK. It has seen relatively positive momentum since the end of 2022, when its share price essentially doubled. But since then, after hitting a peak in early June of last year, the company’s share price tumbled by over 30%. It seems that the hype surrounding the company’s operations was exactly just that. Hype. The management delivered some guidance cuts for FY25, which came in below consensus and surprised many investors, who subsequently went on a selling spree.

The milder weather didn’t help the company’s operations either, but it was much better for humanity's sake not to have as many hurricanes. A lot of the company’s underperformance was out of its hands. If there is no demand, then there is very little you can do to stimulate it. I would say that AI integration helped the company maintain the mid-single-digit growth for now, and without these additional tools, which demand higher margins and pricing, the company’s top-line growth would have been much worse.

I believe that AI in these types of modeling jobs is very beneficial, and the company should continue to improve its offerings going forward. We can see that AI-powered tools like XactAnalysis, Radar, and Hover’s 3D data integration have automated workflow, slashed underwriting cycles, and reduced claims settlement times, which directly improved the company’s adjusted EBITDA growth. It just seems to me that investors are not particularly happy with such a performance. A performance that, if looked at individually, is decent, but if we look at it in broader AI terms, is quite “meh”. Investors seem to be looking for extraordinary improvements in operations, but I don’t think that is very reasonable to assume. The company is already highly profitable, and if it continues to be that way, I don’t see anything wrong with it.

Making shareholders richer through buybacks

The company consistently buys back its own shares every quarter. In just a decade, the company reduced its share count by around 50%, and it repurchased $1.6B worth of shares in the last year. If we look at the graph, that is around 5 million shares repurchased since Q1 ’24 to Q3’25. This aggressive repurchase benefits shareholders by concentrating value among fewer shares, which boosts EPS, ROE (as we saw was massive), and per-share ownership without necessarily diluting quality. However, it is not sufficient to offset the recent share decline completely, but I think in the long-term, once this negative cyclicality turns around, I am expecting to see EPS growth to outpace its historical averages, which should lead to much better share price performance. It will just take patience.

Valuation

Let’s take a look at some projections. Below are the assumptions I used, which, looking at the analysts’ projections, are quite a bit more optimistic.

I think revenue estimates of around 6% CAGR are reasonable, given that is what the company managed to achieve consistently. Operating margins are another topic. I decided to go with essentially EBITDA margins, which is not very conservative of me, but they are achievable, and adjusted metrics are what the company likes to focus on anyway. The rest I went with the company’s historical averages, and its WACC of around 7.6% is sufficient as my discount rate. Below are the EPS projections.

With that said, Verisk’s intrinsic value is around $203 a share, which means it is trading at a slight premium to its fair value.

Investor Takeaway

I believe the company will continue to be quite profitable and most likely command a 30+ P/E ratio in the future. If you already own the company and are happy with what you are getting over the last while, I don’t see a reason to sell. Keep holding it, but as for me, nothing is exciting regarding its mediocre top-line and EPS growth. I don’t think these types of growth numbers are enough to entice me. It helps investors to know that I don’t expect the company to be massively volatile in the long-run, which a lot of investors like about Verisk, and one of the reasons it is trading at such a premium.

Nevertheless, I set a price alert at $180, just in case this share price decline continues. At that point, I would be very tempted to start a position if the fundamentals remain intact.

data.title: Southwest Airlines Q4 Preview: Not A Buy After The Run-Up From The Lows
data.description:
Introduction

Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV) will report at the end of the month (January 29th) its Q4 and full-year numbers, so I thought I’d go over what to expect and what I’m looking for from the report and overall outlook for 2026. Even with a massive improvement in profitability, I am going to have to pass on for now, as it is still rather expensive.

What to Expect

Analysts are expecting the company to do around $0.58 in EPS on $7.5B in sales. This translates to around 8.5% growth sequentially and around 8% y/y. Decent growth, which is expected from the seasonal uptick in holiday travel.

How do these numbers stack up against what the management guided last quarter? They are more or less in line. The management guided 6% capacity growth and 1%-3% RASM increase, which implies total revenue growth to be in the range of 7%-9%, 8% at the midpoint. It is important to note that there have been 12 EPS revisions over the last 90 days.

In terms of whether the company is going to miss or beat these expectations, it is hard to tell. Mainly because of the Government shutdown that happened back in November and early December, which caused a temporary drop in demand in bookings before normalizing. We can see below that the sales numbers tend to miss quite often, which could mean we will see another miss because of the uncertainty.

Overall, it is hard to speculate on what we see in the upcoming quarter report. The first time the company provided guidance was before the company saw any meaningful declines in demand due to the shutdowns, and only at the beginning of December did we get a revised outlook. I am still expecting a decent quarter, to at least match the quiet quarters, but I am also expecting to see much slower growth if we compare to the busy seasons like the summer and the holiday season.

What I’m Looking for

The most important thing I will be focusing on is, of course, the demand trend for this year. I am going to look out for updates on capacity, which I’m assuming we will see modest growth, and an update on fuel prices. Management guided fuel prices to be in the range of $2.20-$2.30 per gallon for Q4. This was apparently too optimistic, because one other main reason for lowering the EBIT guidance to $500m for full-year 2025 is, in fact, higher fuel prices, according to the December 5th 8K filing.

According to some reports, 2026 will see some price easing. IATA puts jet fuel at about $88 per barrel, 2%-3% below 2025 prices. This also equates to around $2.10-$2.15 per gallon globally. So, I expect the management to guide a little EBIT recovery just on lower fuel prices alone, unless the geopolitics somehow get worse over the next year. It’s hard to tell what is going to happen going forward with the Trump Administration. Greenland apparently is still very much in President Trump’s crosshairs. That will most likely lead to further tensions. Oil prices (CL1:COM) are very volatile, so anything is possible.

The company is also going to launch extra legroom options, which will roll out on the 27th of January. This move ends the company’s 50-year open seat policy. I will be listening to how this has progressed so far, and what kind of numbers it did in terms of bookings. The company estimates around $1B incremental increase in EBIT for this year just on assigned seating, and $1.5B run-rate for 2027. This is a massive step up in profitability if that is going to be true. Analysts’ projections after 2025 show the company’s EPS increasing to $3.22 at the midpoint.

Back in Q3, the management already noted a strong interest in new products, saying that ancillary seat buy-ups and fare upgrades were “on track.” We will see how this turned out, but I am also very surprised that it took them this long to roll such a thing out. Retrofitting of the planes is well on its way, so I am interested to see how this is going to develop going forward. The company aims to retrofit its 737-700s by mid-27, with long-haul planes taking priority. We’ll see if they will keep to the plan.

Is LUV a buy ahead of earnings?

Let’s take a look at the company’s valuation model now that we may see a massive improvement in profitability. Below are the assumptions I used for my model.

I kept it simple in terms of the model and only focused on the company’s improved profitability. Also, for revenues, I don’t think the company’s historical average is anywhere near correct to be used. Analysts see mid-single digit growth going forward, so 6% across the board is reasonable. Operating margins start at around 10% turns out to be close to what analysts expect the company to make for 2026. From then on, I decided to improve it by 100bps every year. The rest of the assumptions are historical figures that the company achieved in the long run. I also used LUV’s WACC of around 8.9% as my discount rate, which I think is sufficient. Additionally, I went with 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. Below are the EPS projections we get.

With that said, is LUV a buy at these levels? The fair value we get is around $27.40, so in short, no.

Closing Comments

Even with such an improvement in profitability, the company is still rather expensive. I would like to see a much better improvement across the board before I would consider the company good enough to add to my portfolio.

We may see its share price continue to climb, but I don’t think the move will be based on fundamentals, but rather on sentiment. Less than a year ago, we saw the company’s share price below $25 a share, so it is not out of the realm of possibility that we may see it again if the sentiment once again shifts negative and the company misses on a few quarters.

data.title: BlackRock: Q4 Shows This Giant's Growth Is Far From Over
data.description:
I haven't provided an update for BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) since the middle of last year, but it seems that my buy rating has worked out just fine, with the stock up around 17% since then. While that article focused on the technicals, solid fundamentals and an attractive valuation were also key reasons why I remained bullish on the stock. BlackRock reported their latest earnings recently (Jan 15th), and as of this writing, the stock is up over 6%, so it seems that the firm continues to head in the right direction.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Q4 was a robust quarter for BlackRock. Net flows are generally healthy and financial performance was strong as fees helped to drive growth. In terms of the alternatives space, the firm is throwing down the gauntlet. Fee-paying AUM of the asset class grew 40% YoY, and management stated that they are now targeting $400 billion in private markets fundraising by 2030. This is a clear challenge to alternative management firms and sets up a growth runway for the company. In my view, the strong fundamentals and current opportunities make the valuation feel cheap, and therefore, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on BlackRock.

Reach Is Unparalleled

Before diving into their financials, I believe it is worth mentioning the reach of their business. Investors often say that BlackRock owns the world. I believe the above chart clearly shows that this is true. They are involved in asset classes like cryptocurrencies and alternatives, and they serve all types of clients, big and small. While the company is focused strongly on the U.S., their geographic reach is also vast. This shows that the company has left no stone unturned and that their diversification is strong on many measures. As a result, I would say that the company offers unprecedented levels of stability.

Net Flows Mainly Robust

In terms of net flows, BlackRock saw overall strong results in my view. As a sign, that investors are continuing to flock to their products, you can see that there were $342 billion of net inflows in the quarter. Do note that while the LTM asset growth rate remained unchanged from the previous quarter, the base fee growth rate saw an uptick. Therefore, the company's profitability appears to be robust. More on profitability later.

As a whole, you can see that there is somewhat of a contrast between retail investor optimism and institutional investor caution. While ETF net flows are strong and Retail net flows surged in Q4, there were net outflows in Institutional Index and mild net inflows for Institutional Active. Even though overall net flows are healthy for BlackRock, this may signal that retail investors are now showing some exuberance.

Lastly, note that the firm had a total of $698 billion of inflows in 2025. With Q4 inflows making up nearly half of that, I believe it is quite clear that their business is gaining substantial momentum. BlackRock's AUM now totals an astonishing $14 trillion.

Fees Drive Revenue Growth

For 2025 Q4, BlackRock reported total revenues of $7.008 billion, representing a 23% increase YoY. While that is a slight deceleration from the previous quarter's 25% growth rate, I would say that their business has remained solid. Furthermore, they were able to beat consensus expectations by $308.77 million. Even for a company of their size, this is a good deal of outperformance. Earlier, I mentioned that their fee growth looked strong, and you can see in the above chart that fees were the main driver of the YoY increase in revenues. Base fees are obviously robust, but performance fees were really quite impressive for me, as it shows that their investments are doing well for clients. In the long run, of course, that is important.

Execution Is Strong

As a clear indication that management is executing in a strong manner, profitability is looking good for the firm. For Q4, operating margin was reported to be 45%. While that is actually a 50 basis point dip from the year ago quarter, I would say that investors really can't ask for much more, as an operating margin in the mid-40s percentage range is really quite impressive. Note that back in 2023 Q4, operating margin was just 41.6%. In the quarter, they did see employee compensation and benefits increase by 28% YoY, so perhaps those expenses could be reeled in a bit. For the bottom line, BlackRock reported an adjusted EPS of $13.16. The 10% YoY jump in Q4 is an acceleration from just a 1% increase in Q3, and so there has been notable improvement.

Shareholders Likely Happy

Another reason for shareholders to be happy would be that capital returns have edged higher. For Q4, share repurchases totaled $500 million, which is up 33% YoY. This is contrasted with no YoY change back in Q3. BlackRock also reported that the Board of Directors authorized another 7 million shares for repurchase, so investors can expect buybacks to continue in a strong manner.

As for the dividend, BlackRock maintained their $5.21 quarterly dividend for the entirety of 2025. While Seeking Alpha shows that the yield is a bit low, they are increasing their dividend for 2026. In the above chart, you can see that for 2025, the firm increased the quarterly dividend by just 2% over 2024 levels. For 2026, they announced that they will increase it by 10% from 2025 levels. Therefore, starting in March, shareholders will receive a cash dividend of $5.73 per share. That is a clear step up in capital distribution, and income seekers may find that to be attractive.

Clear Alternatives Push

When looking at asset growth, you can clearly see that the company is making a significant push into alternative assets. Clients assets increased by 49% YoY while fee-paying AUM increased by 40%. This far outpaces the company-wide AUM increase of 22% YoY and so investors should see this asset class as one of the leading growth drivers for BlackRock in the years to come. A few decades ago, BlackRock essentially spun off from Blackstone (BX). Today, the former seems to have intentions of coming for the latter's lunch. For BlackRock investors, that may be promising.

Valuation Has Room To Expand

While BlackRock's forward P/E ratio has rebounded a bit to start 2026, the multiple is not at particularly elevated levels. I believe this shows that the risk/reward is favorable for investors right now.

In today's analysis, BlackRock has been determined to be rock solid. Net flows were overall strong, fee growth powered results, and impressive operating margins showed that execution by management is strong. When I discussed their alternatives push above, I failed to mention that the company is targeting $400 billion in private markets fundraising by 2030. This target clearly shows off their ambitions and given how lucrative the alternatives space can be, I would say that BlackRock's prospects are as bright as ever.

As a result, I believe that the forward P/E ratio still has room to expand from here. The valuation is currently likely pricing in strong fundamentals, but the opportunities ahead call for multiple expansion in my view.

Risks

While BlackRock is diversified in terms of its asset classes, equities and fixed-income make up a majority of their AUM and base fees currently. Why is that a risk? Well, looking forward, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell is set to step down. It is a concern that the President will select a replacement that is loyal to him and that has the potential to diminish Fed independence. Since the President has continuously called for lower rates, there is significant risk that the Fed may cut rates inappropriately with inflation still rather sticky. If this were to result in a rebound in inflation down the road, there is considerable risk that equities and fixed-income will perform in a poor manner. If that is the case, some of BlackRock's fees may come under some pressure. While Fed decisions involve much more than just the Chair's view, there is definitely some political uncertainty, and investors should keep that in mind.

Concluding Remarks

BlackRock's AUM and reach are already awe-inspiring, but there are no signs that the company is done expanding. As stated above, Q4 was a good quarter for the firm in many regards, but perhaps the most exciting development of all is their target for private markets. As a result, in the next few years, investors can expect continued inflows, stronger fees, and likely higher capital returns as well. For that good of an outlook, I would say that the forward P/E still has a decent amount of space to expand, and so I'm reiterating my buy rating on BlackRock.

data.title: Comstock Resources: 2026 Cash Burn Could Be Significant
data.description:
Comstock Resources, Inc. (CRK) may end up with significant cash burn in 2026 if it attempts to maintain production levels while continuing to devote significant resources to the Western Haynesville.

Natural gas strip prices for 2026 are now around $3.30, significantly down from the $4+ level from when I last looked at Comstock. I project that Comstock could end up with nearly $300 million in 2026 cash burn if it attempts to maintain production levels around 1,200 MMCFE per day.

Comstock is able to handle that potential cash burn after its recent divestitures paid down most of its credit facility debt. However, while it has made progress with its Western Haynesville assets, those assets are still fairly uncompetitive with legacy Haynesville assets in terms of economics.

I now estimate Comstock's value at $13.75 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment, which is a slight reduction due to the lower 2026 strip prices.

Current Hedges

Comstock is well hedged for natural gas in 2026, with swaps and collars that cover around 65% of its production (if it averages 1,200 MMCFE per day). These hedges have a floor of approximately $3.50, so Comstock is significantly protected against further drops in natural gas prices. The current 2026 strip is around $3.30 and futures for nine months in 2026 are below $3.50 right now.

Comstock was unhedged beyond 2026 at the last report. The natural gas strip for 2027 is currently around $3.70, so Comstock could add hedges around that level if it chose to.

Potential 2026 Outlook

I have modeled Comstock's 2026 production at 1,200 MMCFE per day, which is approximately its Q3 2025 production after adjusting for its recent divestitures. At current 2026 strip prices (including $3.30 NYMEX natural gas), that results in a projection of $1.394 billion in revenues after hedges.

Natural gas prices have fallen due to robust production levels (and uncooperative weather throughout much of the start to winter) resulting in relatively high storage levels. I continue to believe that $3.75 NYMEX natural gas is a reasonable balance point between supply and demand.

Type

Barrels/Mcf

Realized $ Per Barrel/Mcf

Revenue ($ Million)

Oil (Barrels)

$0

Hedge Value

$79

Total

$1,394

I have assumed that Comstock spends $1.15 billion in capex to maintain production at around 1,200 MMCFE per day in this scenario. This involves a mix of Western Haynesville and legacy Haynesville development. This is slightly higher than Comstock's 2025 budget, which was not enough to maintain production levels.

$1,690

This results in a projection of $296 million in 2026 cash burn for Comstock if it attempts to maintain production with a mix (50/50 rig allocation) of Western Haynesville and legacy Haynesville development.

While Comstock has made strides in the Western Haynesville, due to the high D&C costs, the economics are still considerably worse there than with legacy Haynesville development. Rystad Energy estimates a Western Haynesville breakeven price that is around 30% to 45% higher than legacy Haynesville wells at current costs.

Comstock could reduce its cash burn by accepting some more production declines (its production has been declining for a couple of years) and/or shifting more development towards its legacy Haynesville assets.

Projected Debt Position

Comstock had $580 million in credit facility debt at the end of Q3 2025. It also had $19 million in cash on hand at that time. Comstock divested Shelby Trough assets in Q4 2025 for $430 million and may have had around $40 million in cash burn in Q4 2025. Thus, that would result in a projected $171 million in credit facility debt (net of cash) at the end of 2025.

The projected 2026 cash burn would bring its credit facility debt up to $467 million at the end of 2026 and its net debt up to $3.039 billion, with leverage of 2.9x.

Although Comstock has a relatively high amount of leverage, its debt shouldn't be a problem due to the support of the Jones family. That support (if needed) may come at the cost of dilution, though.

Notes On Valuation

I previously estimated Comstock's value at $14 per share at long-term (after 2026) $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. I am trimming its estimated value by $0.25 per share to $13.75 per share to reflect weaker near-term natural gas prices.

Even though Comstock has around 65% of its 2026 natural gas production hedged, the decline in 2026 strip prices since I last looked at Comstock still results in increased projected cash burn.

Comstock has done a lot of work to delineate and prove out its Western Haynesville assets, but even with some cost reductions over time, the economics there still look relatively mediocre at the $3.50 to $4.00 NYMEX natural gas prices that producers are prepared for. There also still appears to be decades of legacy Haynesville inventory at noticeably lower breakeven prices than the Western Haynesville.

There were some reported insider sales recently, with CEO Jay Allison and CFO Roland Burns selling a combined 71,406 shares for a total of approximately $1.7 million (at an average price of $23.92 per share). These sales only represented approximately 2% of their holdings, though.

Jerry Jones has invested more than $1 billion into Comstock before, but typically at much lower prices. His last purchase was in August 2024 at under $12 per share.

Conclusion

Natural gas strip prices for 2026 have declined to $3.30, resulting in a projection that Comstock will have $296 million in cash burn if it wants to maintain production while devoting half of its rigs to the Western Haynesville.

Despite progress over the years, the Western Haynesville is still fairly capital inefficient compared to legacy Haynesville assets. The legacy Haynesville assets can generate decent returns at the $3.50 to $4.00 natural gas prices that some producers are planning for as a mid-cycle price, but the Western Haynesville requires higher prices.

I do believe that the Western Haynesville is worth exploring, but Comstock's heavy focus on that area is resulting in significant cash burn and may not pay off for many years until much of the lower breakeven natural gas inventory is used up.

I now estimate Comstock's value at $13.75 per share in a long-term $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment. Comstock's liquidity is still fine, so its stock can potentially stay at elevated levels for a long time, but it remains overpriced compared to the estimated value of its assets.

data.title: Ingles Markets: This Discount Won't Last
data.description:
One company that the market continues to underestimate severely, in my opinion, is Ingles Markets, Incorporated (IMKTA). This is a firm that operates as a small grocery chain, and with a market capitalization of only $1.31 billion as of this writing, it is often overlooked by market participants. Management has not been really focused on growth. And in fact, over the last fiscal year, revenue and profits declined.

Despite this, shares of the business are trading at ridiculously low levels, not only in relation to cash flows and revenue but also in relation to book value. This is true on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar enterprises. And it signals to me that, so long as fundamentals don't deteriorate from here, material upside should occur at some point. That is why I have consistently rated the company a "Strong Buy." That is what I last reaffirmed it at when I wrote about it in September of last year. And honestly, I don't see any reason why that assessment should change right now.

Ingles Markets Is Still In The Discount Category

As of this writing, the newest data that investors have regarding Ingles Markets covers through the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $1.37 billion. That was unfortunately down from the $1.40 billion the business reported a year earlier. But this requires some discussion. The fact of the matter is that, back in September of 2024, Hurricane Helene had a significant impact on parts of North Carolina. This is where the company's headquarters are located. And the result was disastrous flooding, power and communication outages, water outages, road closures, and more. This event caused four of its locations to shut down temporarily. And even through the final quarter of 2025, three of them remain closed. In fact, there could be some pain this year and even next year, since the firm is planning staggered reopenings of these locations in 2026 and 2027.

As awful as this is, this is a much better situation than if the firm were experiencing organic revenue declines. There have been some other impacts as well. Management does not provide quarterly data for this, but they do provide some annual data. Comparable sales ended up dropping from 2024 to 2025 to the tune of $168.8 million. There has been some weakness here, specifically because of a reduction in transactions. Fuel revenue is also down, some of which can be chalked up to closed locations and some of which can be attributed to lower fuel costs.

The decline in revenue for the company did not, in fact, cause overall profits to worsen for the final quarter. In fact, the company went from a $1.5 million loss to a $25.7 million profit. But we need to consider here the extreme impacts from weather events. A more accurate measure of profitability would be cash flows. Operating cash flow contracted from $73.2 million to $59.9 million. Stripping out changes in working capital, we get a decline from $69.2 million to $55.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business inched up from $67.5 million to $69 million.

The chart above shows financial results for 2025 in their entirety compared to 2024. And in the image below, you can see the different contributions to the overall change in revenue for the company. Unfortunately, the 2025 fiscal year was worse than 2024 was. And even though there were items that were associated with weather events, there were some comparable-related issues that impacted the company.

As disappointing as these results might have been, we do need to focus on the overall value proposition here. For starters, the overall health of the company seems solid. It had net debt at the end of 2025 of only $150.9 million. That's an amount that's low enough that, in theory, management could pay it off in a year. We also have the other three stores that are still closed that will eventually be reopened. That should boost overall financial performance for the enterprise.

From a valuation standpoint, things look exceptionally positive. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using results from 2024 and 2025. Although the price-to-earnings multiple is nothing to write home about, the other multiples are exceptionally low. In fact, in the same chart, I compared Ingles Markets to five other similar firms. And on both a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, I found that only one of the five companies was cheaper than it is. And that particular player is none other than Albertsons Companies (ACI), a grocery chain that I am also very bullish about.

There are other ways to look at the picture as well. For instance, we can use the price-to-sales multiple. There is a lot more variation here in terms of valuation compared to the cash flow valuation approaches. But in the chart below, you can see that the company is trading at just 0.25 times the revenue that it generated in 2025. Only one of the five grocery chains that I compared it to is cheaper than it is. And not surprisingly, that is Albertsons Companies.

Even more telling of the value proposition that exists for investors, in my view, is the price-to-book multiple. To see a company trade at a discount to book value is always interesting. Sometimes, it is a value trap, driven by excessive leverage or terminal operating issues. But I don't see that being the case here. In the chart below, you can see that Ingles Markets is trading at just 0.43 times book value. This means that, in theory, you could shut the business down, sell off all of its assets, and more than double your money if book value equates to market value. By comparison, none of the other companies come even close. In fact, if Ingles Markets were to trade at the same price-to-book multiple as the cheapest of these five comparable enterprises, it would imply upside for shareholders of 525.6%. That is monumental.

Normally, in cases such as this, there are excessive amounts of leverage. But as I detailed already, the net debt picture of the business is quite modest. And in fact, in the chart below, you can see how the firm's net leverage ratio stands up to these other five players. On this basis, Ingles Markets is better off than any of these enterprises. And it's not just by a small amount. It is a significant margin. This means that the risk profile for investors is also low. If anything, a company such as this, especially when its financial results stabilize, should justify a premium compared to other comparable firms.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, it is my belief that the market continues to underestimate Ingles Markets. The company has had its challenges. Nobody will deny that. However, the stock is one of the cheapest that I have ever seen. This is true not only on an absolute basis but also relative to other similar firms. This justifies, in my opinion, the "Strong Buy" rating that I have historically assigned to the company.

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP): A Cannabis REIT With An 11% Yield | 2-Minute Analysis
data.description:
Follow NewLake Capital Partners on Seeking Alpha!

Learn more about Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings

Get Started With Seeking Alpha Premium Now

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2 Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain, but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. Today's episode is from this request. So, let's throw 2 minutes on the clock and dive-in.

Today, we are looking at NewLake Capital Partners, ticker symbol NLCP. Kicking things off here with the Quant rating system, Strong Buy rating for this REIT here on Seeking Alpha, and we'll dive into some of the metrics here in just a moment.

Jumping over to the Seeking Alpha Analysts here who also have a Strong Buy in aggregate on this REIT. And that's from five analysts in the last 90-days providing coverage. And lastly, Wall Street is a Strong Buy rating on this REIT as well. And that's from two analysts providing coverage in the last 90-days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha Analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

So, let's dive deeper. This is a $314.91 million market capitalization company found within the real estate sector and the industrial REITs industry. Diving into the Valuation grade here is an A+ grade and two notable metrics stick out. First, the price to AFFO is 7.24 compared to 15.46. And the total debt-to-equity is 1.93%, compared to the sector at 95.61%.

The Growth grade is currently B and two things stick out here as well. For one, the AFFO growth year-over-year is 10.05%, compared to the sector at 3.28%. And the revenue growth five year historical CAGR is 112.17%, compared to the sector at 7.83%.

The Profitability grade is currently an A+. AFFO divided by total revenue is 86.30%, compared to the sector at 40.87%. And the net income to total debt is 343.47%, compared to the sector at just 6.48% there.

Checking in, Momentum is currently a B+ grade. That's one year price performance of negative 7.24%, compared to the sector, which is only about down negative 3%. However, three month price performance has this REIT up 17.75%, while the sector is only up 2.17%.

Lastly, Revisions here is an A+ grade. That's two up revisions and zero down revisions for EPS over the last three months and two up revisions and zero down revisions for revenue over the last three months.

Now, with this being a REIT, it does have a nice dividend yield that is 11.42% with a payout ratio of 84.31%. Safety grade here is an A+. The dividend growth grade is a C-. Dividend yield is an A+ and the dividend consistency is a C. We click on that here. That's four years of growth and consecutive years of dividend payments. And if we look at the yield compared to the sector, dividend yield of 11.42% is definitely outperforming the sector at just 4.82%.

Now, I highly encourage everybody to come follow NLCP here on Seeking Alpha, as well as explore any other REIT that you may be interested in and utilize our proprietary REIT grades here on Seeking Alpha. And that's going to follow it up for this episode. If you have a ticker you want us to cover, add it to the comment section below, and don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2 Minute Analysis gets published.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content provided for informational purposes only. Seeking Alpha does not offer any personalized investment advice and is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

data.title: High Demand, Low Supply: The Bullish Case For Commodities
data.description:
Commodities like gold, silver and copper had a strong run in 2025. Hussein Allidina, Head of Commodities with TD Asset Management, says simple supply-demand dynamics could push prices even higher in 2026.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: While many commodities had a strong run in 2025, our featured guest today says simple supply and demand dynamics could push prices even higher in 2026. Hussein Allidina, managing director and head of commodities with TD Asset Management, joins us now with more.

Hussein, great to see you. We all know about the year behind us and how strong it was. Let's talk about the conditions right now and why you still see them as positive for commodities.

Hussein Allidina: Thanks, Greg. And sorry I'm not in the studio with you today. It's very much the story about where we are in the commodity cycle. 12 to 13 years, the commodity space has not attracted capital into the supply side, frankly. And that has contributed to limiting our ability to produce. And demand is growing.

And in 2026, with the reflation trade very much in focus, demand looks like it's going to continue to grow. And that continues to tighten balances and support prices. Ultimately, we need to see prices move to levels that encourage the companies to deploy capital. And because commodity supply is inelastic, it's going to take years before that copper production, that oil production comes to market.

Greg Bonnell: I know depending on the commodity, we'll have a different story about what the demand is. But overall, are we talking about a stronger global economy than we thought, new technologies, a bit of hedging? What's the play here?

Hussein Allidina: So the biggest driver of commodity demand, broadly speaking, is economic growth. And with this material focus on ensuring that economic growth not just in the US, but globally, is increasing to make the debt and deficit relative to GDP improve, there's a pronounced focus from both a monetary and a fiscal impulse perspective to drive growth higher.

So that's the big thematic. But if we look under the hood, clearly the desire to build out hyperscalers, data centers, and the required power generation is going to be very constructive for commodities, broadly speaking. And the spend on military and defense is very commodity-intensive. So, I'd argue that your GDP being positive is the biggest driver. And then, in the details, there's very specific things that are going to require a tremendous amount of copper, uranium, natural gas as we move into this new world.

Greg Bonnell: Alright, let's get into those specific things. You mentioned copper. Let's talk about the bullish case for copper.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so copper has moved pretty meaningfully. We're trading north of $13,000 a ton in the LME. Part of this is a reflection of concerns around Section 232 and the magnitude of tariffs, if at all, that we'll see on copper, aluminum, silver, and other strategic materials. That's contributed to bringing supply-- or sucking supply from the rest of the world into the US.

There was some news overnight on Section 232. It doesn't look like a tremendous amount of clarity. But outside of that, Greg, again, GDP growth is very, very supportive for copper demand. And the AI, energy transition theme requires quite a bit of copper.

These hyperscalers, data centers have copper within them. The power that is going to be used to facilitate the growth in data centers requires a tremendous amount of copper. We're seeing aluminum perform as well because there is some industrial substitution that happens as copper prices move higher. But that's the big story on the demand side.

And then on the supply side, we have seen in 2025 a number of mines disappoint. And again, because we've not encouraged CapEx over the course of the last 10 years, when we look at that pipeline in the next three, five, seven years, there's no new major copper projects that are coming to market. And that ultimately, I think, is what lends to this view that the copper market, on a multi-year view, is going to be in deficit.

Greg Bonnell: Alright, that's copper. Let's talk about gold and silver. Gold has been very interesting for a long time, but silver in the last several months, too, catching a lot of attention. What's the story here? What's the setup?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so I'll talk to gold first. And the gold story is very much portfolio diversification on the part of central banks. If we look at emerging market central banks, they have been large buyers of gold, broadly speaking since 2021, following the confiscation of Afghan and then, following that, Russian reserves by the US government.

If you are an emerging market central bank sitting on 80%, 90% of your reserves in fiat, in paper, I think there is some desire to diversify into gold. And we're seeing that. Those emerging market central banks have been buying over 1,000 tons of gold per annum. For perspective, Greg, we produce about 2,500 tons of gold annually. So that's been a big source of demand. And we don't think that that changes anytime in the near future.

And then you have, obviously, seen some interest on the part of institutional and retail to participate in gold. And you can see that by looking at the amount of open interest that sits in the ETFs that track gold. Those ETFs are still well below-- from a open interest perspective, well below the highs that we've seen in recent years. So there is still, I think, some additional fodder there.

Silver-- a very different story. Silver is not being bought by central banks. It is being bought by investors, for sure. There's a larger industrial component to silver. But if I look at the balance, the silver balance, it's in deficit. And I'm going to sound like a broken record, but I'm not encouraged. The production of silver-- if we look at the largest producer of silver in the world, Mexico, their production is declining. And it's going to take time for us to grow supply in a meaningful way.

Now, I just want to caution-- silver is something that has moved meaningfully over the course of the last 6 to 12 months, and in particular over the course of the last six to eight weeks. We could see silver up another 20%, down another 20%. It's not something that we are actively participating in in our fund.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about oil because, obviously, we're seeing some whipsaw action from day to day based on geopolitical tensions today. We're down almost 5% on a barrel of crude. How does an investor start to think about crude in this environment?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah so, we can address it in two parts. There's the fundamental supply-and-demand balance. And concurrently, we've got the geopolitical supply-side risk. Fundamentally, balances should be, on paper, over supply through 2026 in the tune of somewhere between 1 and 2 million barrels a day. This is something the market has been anticipating for a very long time.

Admittedly, the builds and inventories that we have seen globally have not occurred in commercial centers. So you're not seeing it in the Department of Energy data in the US. You're not seeing it in the ARA data out of Europe. You are seeing material builds and inventories, but those inventories are being held by China. It's gone into the Chinese SPR. And they do have room to continue to build, should they choose to do so.

We're also seeing a lot of oil sitting on water. This is part of the reflection of the sanctions. Oil has been stranded, particularly in the Far East. So you're seeing more oil sitting on water. Ultimately, that should come to market. But it's unclear if China, again, continues to buy given the geopolitical landscape. We've seen Venezuela disappear very much as a source for China.

Now, on the geopolitical front, and the reason that crude is rallying and has been rallying over the course of the last week, is right around the situation in Iran, which is a horrible situation-- fourth-largest producer of oil globally. They produce 3 million barrels a day, they export a million barrels a day. Unclear how that situation evolves, but the market is pricing in some risk around a supply disruption.

Greg, we've talked about this before. Over the course of the last 10, 12 years, we've not had any meaningful supply-side disruption around geopolitics. So I think the market's a bit benign on this, notwithstanding the fact that prices have increased. If there were to be a disruption, price would be materially higher than where it is today.

Greg Bonnell: You put all that together from an investor perspective, commodities have been grabbing a lot of headlines. What kind of role can they play in a portfolio?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so we've argued for a while. Academics and those that are students of portfolio construction will tell you that because commodities-- the derivative-- behaves differently than the stuff that we hold in our portfolio today-- equities, fixed income-- you want an allocation of 5% to 10% because it's diversifying, because it is one of the few assets that is positively correlated with inflation, so it acts as an inflation hedge.

And then on top of all that, Greg, given that the commodity space has not seen investment over the course of the last 10 to 15 years, we're in a very unique period for the commodity market where prices will have to move higher in an effort to attract capital. And again, we're competing today with AI that's taking a lot of that capital. AI itself is very commodity-intensive.

For the next five, seven years, we do see commodity returns averaging well above their long-term average of 5.4%. And we think that portfolios that have this 5-10% allocation to commodities will not only outperform those that don't, but the drawdown, historically, of a portfolio that has commodities is shallower than the portfolio that doesn't.

Original Post

data.title: IYK: Consumer Staples Dashboard For January
data.description:
This article offers a top-down view of the consumer staples sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help to analyze sector ETFs such as The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLP) and iShares US Consumer Staples ETF (IYK), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield (“EY”), Sales Yield (“SY”), Free Cash Flow Yield (“FY”), Return on Equity (“ROE”), Gross Margin (“GM”). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY, and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales, and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the “something” is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value And Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score (“VS”) is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score (“QS”) is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Value And Quality Chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industry (higher is better).

Evolution Since Last Month

The value score has deteriorated in staple/food retail, beverages, and household products.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum scores based on median returns.

Interpretation

The beverage industry is greatly undervalued relative to 11-year averages, and its quality score is close to the historical baseline. Food, and to a lesser extent personal care products, are also undervalued. Tobacco is the least compelling subsector from a fundamental point of view, with the lowest value and quality scores.

IYK Fast Facts

The iShares US Consumer Staples ETF was launched on 6/12/2000 and tracks the Russell 1000 Consumer Staples RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index. IYK has a portfolio of 54 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.69%, and a total expense ratio of 0.38%, while XLP charges only 0.08%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

As described in the prospectus by iShares, the underlying index invests in constituents of the Russell 1000 index classified in consumer staples, with weights based on float-adjusted capitalization, capped at 22.5% in any constituent and 45% in aggregate in those exceeding 4.5%.

The portfolio is very concentrated, with significant risks related to the largest companies. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 67.4% of asset value, and the four names with heavy positions weigh between 8% and 14%.

With higher historical returns than XLP, IYK is well-suited for long-term investors seeking exposure in consumer non-cyclical companies. However, higher daily volumes make XLP a better trading instrument. Both funds are quite concentrated in the top holdings. Investors seeking a balanced portfolio in the sector will prefer Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF (RSPS).

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a food company with an earnings yield above 0.0427 (or price/earnings below 23.42) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A dashboard list is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

LW

It is part of a rotational model with a positive bias on the long term, not the result of an analysis of each stock.
